steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content: The blank introduces a noun phrase (title + name) that serves as the subject, positioned directly before the verb "found."

steps.title: Apply the subject-verb rule
steps.content:
No punctuation separates a subject from its verb. The subject here is "Entomologist Heather Grab" and the verb is "found." The title "Entomologist" and the name "Heather Grab" form a single noun phrase — no comma separates a title from the name it precedes.

A — "Entomologist Heather Grab:": A colon after the subject before the verb is incorrect — colons introduce lists or elaborations, not verbs. Fails.

B — "Entomologist, Heather Grab,": Commas around "Heather Grab" suggest it is a nonessential appositive that could be removed, but the name is essential to identify who found the results. Also inserts punctuation between subject and verb. Fails.

C — "Entomologist Heather Grab": No punctuation between the subject and the verb "found." Correct.

D — "Entomologist Heather Grab,": A comma between the subject and verb is not allowed in standard English. Fails.
